channel.description: Sporadic musings and technical commentary from Torsten Scholak, a Canada-based theoretical physicist.

item.title: Log-Likelihood Ratio (LLR) in The Context of Recommendations
item.dc:creator: Torsten Scholak
item.description:
Looking at all recorded history, do the events $A$ and $B$ co-occur more often than chance would suggest?

If the events $A$ and $B$ occur independently, then \begin{align} p(a, b) = p(a) p(b) \end{align} where $a \in \{A, \not A\}$, $b \in \{B, \not B\}$.

Furthermore, \begin{align} p(a|B) = \frac{p(a, B)}{p(B)} = p(a) = \frac{p(a, \not B)}{p(\not B)} = p(a|\not B) \end{align}

item.title: Test
item.dc:creator: Torsten Scholak
item.description:
Literature

This is a preliminary list of articles and books with necessary background information. Annotations and summaries will follow where necessary.

Where To Start Reading?

I recommend starting with Russ Salakhutdinov's article about Bayesian probabilistic matrix factorization. Alex Beutel's article should be next. The issue of side information should be postponed since it adds further complications.

Most of imm's code is based on the approach described in Dilan Görür's article. The inference algorithms used by the imm package are introduced and explained in the five articles in the section "Bayesian Inference Algorithms". Perhaps most interesting is the last article in that section: Ge et al. describe a distributed approach to inference for Dirichlet process mixture models.

Papers

Recommender Systems Based On Matrix Factorization

Without Side Information

  1. Salakhutdinov, Ruslan, and Andriy Mnih, 2008, "Bayesian Probabilistic Matrix Factorization Using Markov Chain Monte Carlo," in In ICML ’08: Proceedings Of the 25th International Conference on Machine Learning. doi:10.1145/1390156.1390267. Available at: https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~amnih/papers/bpmf.pdf.

  2. Sutskever Ilya, Ruslan Salakhutdinov, and Joshua B. Tenenbaum, 2009, "Modelling relational data using Bayesian clustered tensor factorization," in Advances In Neural Information Processing Systems 22, edited by Y. Bengio, D. Schuurmans, J. D. Lafferty, C. K. I. Williams, and A. Culotta (Curran Associates, Inc.), pp. 1821–1828. Available at: http://papers.nips.cc/paper/3863-modelling-relational-data-using-bayesian-clustered-tensor-factorization.pdf.

  3. Beutel, Alex, Kenton Murray, Christos Faloutsos, and Alexander J. Smola, 2014, "CoBaFi: Collaborative Bayesian Filtering," in Proceedings Of the 23rd International Conference on World Wide Web. WWW ’14 (ACM, New York, NY, USA), pp. 97–108. doi:10.1145/2566486.2568040. Available at: http://alexbeutel.com/papers/www2014.cobafi.pdf.

With Side Information

  1. Porteous, Ian, Arthur U. Asuncion, and Max Welling, 2010, "Bayesian Matrix Factorization With Side Information and Dirichlet Process Mixtures.," in AAAI, edited by Maria Fox and David Poole (AAAI Press). Available at: http://www.aaai.org/ocs/index.php/AAAI/AAAI10/paper/view/1871/2039.

  2. Park, Sunho, Yong-Deok Kim, and Seungjin Choi, 2013, "Hierarchical Bayesian Matrix Factorization With Side Information," in Proceedings Of the Twenty-Third International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence. IJCAI ’13 (AAAI Press), pp. 1593–1599. Available at: http://mlg.postech.ac.kr/~seungjin/publications/ijcai13_ParkSH.pdf.

  3. Kim, Yong-Deok, and Seungjin Choi, 2014, "Scalable Variational Bayesian Matrix Factorization With Side Information," in Proceedings Of the Seventeenth International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics, pp. 493–502. Available at: http://jmlr.org/proceedings/papers/v33/kim14b.pdf

  4. Wang, Hao, Naiyan Wang, and Dit-Yan Yeung, 2015, "Collaborative Deep Learning For Recommender Systems," in Proceedings Of the 21th ACM SIGKDD International Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining. KDD ’15 (ACM, New York, NY, USA), pp. 1235–1244. doi:10.1145/2783258.2783273. Preprint available at: http://arxiv.org/pdf/1409.2944.pdf.

  5. Li, Sheng, Jaya Kawale, and Yun Fu, 2015, "Deep Collaborative Filtering Via Marginalized Denoising Auto-Encoder," in Proceedings Of the 24th ACM International on Conference on Information and Knowledge Management. CIKM ’15 (ACM, New York, NY, USA), pp. 811–820. doi:10.1145/2806416.2806527. Preprint available at: http://arxiv.org/pdf/1409.2944v2.pdf.

Applied Bayesian Statistics

Nonparametric Bayesian Statistics

  1. Rasmussen, Carl Edward, 2000, "The Infinite Gaussian Mixture Model," in Advances In Neural Information Processing Systems, pp. 554–560. Available at: https://www.seas.harvard.edu/courses/cs281/papers/rasmussen-1999a.pdf.

  2. Görür, Dilan, and Carl Edward Rasmussen, 2010, "Dirichlet Process Gaussian Mixture Models: Choice Of the Base Distribution," Journal of Computer Science and Technology. doi:10.1007/s11390-010-9355-8.

  3. Miller, Jeffrey W, and Matthew T Harrison, 2015, "Mixture Models with a Prior on the Number of Components," arXiv preprint arXiv:1502.06241. Preprint available at: http://www2.stat.duke.edu/~jwm40/publications/MFM.pdf.

Bayesian Inference Algorithms

MCMC

  1. Neal, Radford M., 2000, "Markov Chain Sampling Methods for Dirichlet Process Mixture Models," Journal of Computational and Graphical Statistics 9, pp. 249–265. doi:10.2307/1390653. Preprint available at: http://www.cs.toronto.edu/~radford/ftp/mixmc.pdf.

Split And Merge

  1. Jain, Sonia, and Radford M. Neal, 2004, "A Split-Merge Markov Chain Monte Carlo Procedure for the Dirichlet Process Mixture Model," J. Comput. Graph. Statist. 13, pp. 158–182. doi:10.1198/1061860043001. Available at: http://www.cs.toronto.edu/~radford/ftp/mixsplit.pdf.

  2. Dahl, David B., 2003, "An Improved Merge-Split Sampler for Conjugate Dirichlet Process Mixture Models." Technical Report 1086 (Department of Statistics, University of Wisconsin). Available at: http://www.stat.wisc.edu/techreports/tr1086.pdf

  3. Jain, Sonia, and Radford M. Neal, 2007, "Splitting And Merging Components of a Nonconjugate Dirichlet Process Mixture Model," Bayesian Anal. 2 (International Society for Bayesian Analysis), pp. 445–472. doi:10.1214/07-BA219. Available at: http://www.cs.toronto.edu/~radford/ftp/mixsplit2.pdf.

Distributed Slice Sampling

  1. Ge, Hong, Yutian Chen, Moquan Wan, and Zoubin Ghahramani, 2015, "Distributed Inference For Dirichlet Process Mixture Models," in Proceedings Of the 32nd International Conference on Machine Learning. Vol. 37. JMLR Proceedings (JMLR.org), pp. 2276–2284. Available at: http://jmlr.org/proceedings/papers/v37/gea15.pdf.

item.title: Chipgate
item.dc:creator: Torsten Scholak
item.description: If you read this, you probably heard the claim that the battery performance of the new iPhones is markedly different depending on whether its system on chip (SoC) is manufactured by TSMC or by Samsung. Early reports indicated that the TSMC chip allows for a dramatically better battery lifetime than its Samsung counterpart. Supposedly, this is due to TSMC's slight advantage in the semiconductor device fabrication process, which gives the TSMC chip a competitive edge -- although both iPhone chips are based on the same Apple A9 design and despite the fact that Samsung uses a smaller 14 nm process.

item.title: Collaborative Bayesian Filtering
item.dc:creator: Torsten Scholak
item.description: I recently found this paper: Beutel et al. (2014). Want to reproduce the model and the results.

item.title: A Dockerized Login Server for Docker Services
item.dc:creator: Torsten Scholak
item.description: I needed a way to securely access my MongoDB instance running in one of my Docker containers from the stupendous wilds of the Internet. This had to overcome the fact that the server with the MongoDB instance is behind a firewall with NAT.

item.title: A $K$-Means Odyssey
item.dc:creator: Torsten Scholak
item.description:
In this article, I tell you how I

  1. implemented two versions of the tf-idf statistic,

  2. applied these statistics to a certain consumer-item transaction dataset,

  3. created the spherical mini-batch $k$-means implementation that you can find on my GitHub repository,

  4. deployed spherical $k$-means clustering on the consumer-item data, and

  5. trained models with different $k$.

I call this an odyssey, because, as I was exploring, coding, and waiting for results, it occurred to me that I was lost on a journey to nowhere, and that, ultimately, all this wouldn't help me with my project (for reasons explained here). Yet, I was compelled to write about this futile adventure, because someone -- maybe you! -- might find it useful.

item.title: Big-Data Work-Flow with MongoDB
item.dc:creator: Torsten Scholak
item.description: For my current project, I need to be able to routinely handle tens of gigabytes of data. After a failed attempt to create a convenient work-flow with HDF5 on a single machine, I decided to store everything in a MongoDB database on my server and to have my desktop retrieving only the data that I need. I'm happy to report that I have finally concocted a system that works.

item.title: The Setup
item.dc:creator: Torsten Scholak
item.description: In the style of an interview on usesthis.com, I want to give you some info about the hard- and software I'm currently using for my projects. I'll also tell you how to deploy some of that software.

item.title: Communities and Markets
item.dc:creator: Torsten Scholak
item.description:
I have been fairly quiet as of late. This is not because nothing has happened, but rather because I have been (and still am) busy working on a data science project in which I try something new and exciting. Recently, I was out of town for a couple of days traveling the Maritimes, and I found some time to reflect and to start writing on a series of articles about this particular project. This is now the first.

In my new project, I am studying the segmentation of large markets by identifying communities of consumers with similar shopping habits. This is an effort to improve recommendation systems and to allow for better business opportunities.
